title: Mixing pig groups safely
subtitle: Reducing aggression and stress when introducing pigs to each other.
excerpt: Pigs that don't know each other will fight to establish dominance when mixed. Some fighting is unavoidable and normal; severe fighting that leaves pigs injured or unable to eat is not. Here's how to set up introductions to limit the aggression, what to watch for, and when to intervene.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. AHDB Pork, pig health and management guidance. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young female pig that has not yet had a litter.
The dominance ranking within a group of pigs; established through fighting when groups first mix, and maintained through body language thereafter.

content.text: When pigs meet strangers, they establish a

through a sequence of encounters: nose-to-nose contact, parallel walking with shoulder shoving, and then brief but intense biting and head-slamming. In a well-managed introduction, this lasts a few hours and settles. In a poorly managed one, it continues for days and leaves pigs with wounds, weight loss, and in extreme cases, death.

Mix pigs in the early evening if possible, they're more settled after a day of feeding, and darkness reduces visibility and aggression intensity. Mixing at first light into an empty stomach makes fighting worse.

Introduce pigs in a pen that neither group has occupied before. Moving resident pigs into a new space and adding new pigs at the same time removes the territorial element. Moving new pigs into a pen already occupied by resident pigs heavily favours the residents.
Match pigs by size and weight as closely as possible. A
at 40 kg mixed with a finisher at 90 kg is going to lose badly. A size difference of more than 20% within the group is a risk factor for injury.

Spray all pigs with the same strong-smelling substance, a squirt of pig scent spray, a splash of cider vinegar, or a rub with straw from both groups' bedding. This confuses the nose-based identification that drives aggression.
Provide at least two feeding points. That way the lowest-ranked pig can eat without standing next to the dominant one.
Provide a solid barrier, a board, a bale, or a feed bin, that a smaller pig can get behind and break the line of sight during an escalating conflict.

Stand by for the first 30 minutes of the introduction. Break up fights that persist for more than two minutes without a break, or where one pig cannot escape and is being bitten repeatedly about the head and flanks. Separate a pig that draws blood and keep it isolated for 24 hours before trying again. Don't separate pigs and then reintroduce them into the same group the same day, the hierarchy needs resetting, and re-introduction into an established group is worse than the original mixing.
